transcript:
Danny Nelson: All right. So Kash, welcome up and we're going to get things started today. I want to talk about where Jupiter is in its story and uh like how this is appealing to the institutional investor. Take that word however you will, but tell me about it.

Kash Dhanda: Yeah, I think we're working with institutions in a few different factors, right? So we think about those who are first capital allocators and looking to deploy on-chain in a various uh number of kind of products. A lot of them are looking at the tokenized money market funds or how they can use stablecoins and so forth. The second way is those who are looking for yield. And so Jupiter Lend, our borrow-lend protocol, has been popular in that regard along with JupSOL, which is our uh, you know, industry-leading LST. Uh, but the one that's I think the most exciting is actually those who are doing product integrations, right? So not just the capital but those who have their own clientele, their own audience that they want to start to offer trading and other opportunities to. And there we're seeing the all the Jupiter APIs just get adopted left, right, and center.

Danny Nelson: Now, you say that it's a superapp, but if it don't you have a risk of spreading yourself out too thin? Like, how can you make sure that you're not just doing a lot of things but maybe not any one of them too well?

Kash Dhanda: Yeah, it is indeed a risk. I think the way that we solve for it is uh an organizational design. So at Jupiter, we really try to embrace this idea of an ecosystem of founders, right? Where we have uh we've grown largely by M&A so far rather than natural hiring. I think we've acquired about eight companies. Just announced that we acquired Rain.fi earlier today. And we take these companies, they've been working together for years, very tight-knit, very strong CEOs, and then make them the product lead for whatever they're working on. So, you know, uh Guam from Sonar Watch is crushing it on portfolio, right? 3 million uh users per quarter and so forth. Uh, Quentin and the team from Rain.fi are going to be working on Offer Book, our new peer-to-peer lending software and so on. Uh, so really it's about ensuring that the right people are there who are used to working autonomously. Uh, I sometimes compare this to like early America where we have very strong states' rights uh at Jupiter, where the states are the products, and then there's a thin federal layer at the top that helps ensure that things kind of are communicated effectively and coordinated and so on. It's not easy. There's definitely uh difficulties as we know, but uh it is the best way we know to ship product faster than anyone else can.

Danny Nelson: Uh, so today we had a big piece of news coming out of Jupiter with the hiring of a new president from KKR. Tell us a little bit about that.

Kash Dhanda: Yeah, uh so his name is Xiao Xiao. I hope I pronounce that correctly, Xiao Xiao. I apologize apologize if I didn't.

Danny Nelson: I was hoping you would say it instead of me, but uh...

Kash Dhanda: Yeah. We call him XX uh internally. Uh, and indeed, he was leading digital asset strategy at KKR, which is a $700 billion uh private equity fund uh for something like the last five years. Before that, Boston Consulting Group. And he is, I think, really emblematic of what we're starting to see in crypto. Where two or three years ago, the real goal was how do we get the best devs from Web2, fintech startups, SF area, etc., to come on-chain and start to build applications. That has been largely successful as we've seen from a number of the great companies on Solana now. The next step is how do we get people that can, you know, speak multiple languages and bring that depth of expertise in terms of what the problems that need to be solved are by a new set of users. I would say Jupiter, uh I hope this is not contentious to say, we've done very well with retail audiences, right? We're among the most used protocols in all of crypto. Uh, and now we're starting to kind of transition and start to grow into this new audience. And so uh he will be essential for that. And I think that he will not be the last kind of major hire that's making the jump. Uh, it seems that a lot of people at these kind of like larger TradFi institutions, they can see the writing on the wall, right? They know that the future of finance is going to be built with on-chain rails, and they want to be deep in the action rather than uh, you know, kind of further aside on the periphery.

Danny Nelson: Now, when you're scaling up and moving from that simply retail audience to the big money, the big traders, uh the the the things that you need to pay attention to change. And I think the lessons that we take from TradFi change too. Like, we're rebuilding financial systems here. Uh, that doesn't mean that everything that exists in the TradFi world is bad or is good. It it mean we there's probably opportunities to learn from that. So I'd love to hear your thoughts on on what we can take away from the TradFi world, what Jupiter needs to do as well.

Kash Dhanda: Yeah, I've uh I've been fairly consistent in this idea that, you know, on Crypto Twitter, there's a lot of talk of like DeFi will replace TradFi entirely or some, you know, some antagonistic kind of relationship. And I think that that is uh good for getting engagement on Twitter and probably not descriptive of what the future is going to be. It's not DeFi versus TradFi. It's going to be DeFi plus TradFi in this kind of unified finance uh approach. And so at Jupiter, we have a lot to learn in terms of uh, you know, we're very good at this kind of B2C model of finding people, getting them to stay really sticky because the product suite is so great, like Jupiter Mobile and many of these others. Uh, and now we need to kind of focus more on the hand-to-hand combat with some of the bigger guys, right? Uh, the advantage of trying to move into this new audience, uh let's say large-scale credit funds for Jupiter Lend, is that there is a short list of, you know, call it 50 funds around the world that really matter there. And you can just make the list and start to go after it. But you need to build this kind of, you know, business development muscle, the storytelling muscle that's a little bit different than the broad go-to-market strokes that are required for Jupiter's success so far. And again, that's where Xiao Xiao in particular comes in, but the rest of the team is also kind of upskilling and learning here. Uh, I think the other thing that we can learn uh from TradFi uh so to speak is how to uh tell the story really concisely. I mean, this is another kind of difficulty with Jupiter if I'm being very honest. You know, we have 12 revenue-generating products across web, mobile, APIs, all these different things. Uh, and it's hard to get like a summary three or four line statement. I was listening to um I think it was Matt Hougan on a podcast and he's like the average...

Danny Nelson: Yeah, he's great.

Kash Dhanda: Uh, he was uh he made a great point that like, hey, a lot of these financial advisors, they're spending 300 minutes a week on uh, you know, portfolio construction, and crypto is something like 1% of their portfolio. So you're getting three minutes a week that they're thinking about crypto. Which means you cannot have a 10-page long kind of thesis on the inner workings of RFQ systems and so forth. Instead, you have to get really refined in how you tell that story in a way that's intelligible to this kind of new audience.

Danny Nelson: Yeah, that's a very good point. Uh at Bitwise when we're dealing with how to talk about these things. You only have a certain amount of time to really get big ideas across. And the challenge is making making things legible, making things understandable to the broadest audience without losing the meaning inside of it. Uh, one place that I'm really looking toward is what's happening in the US around uh DeFi technology like smart contracts and automated market makers. We see the SEC now basically encouraging builders not only to um not have to worry about getting sued, but sca scale up what they're doing and try to integrate these into traditional financial ecosystems. I'm wondering, is Jupiter looking toward that world and how?

Kash Dhanda: Yes, absolutely. So we have the benefit of uh owning most of the kind of organic trading flows on Solana and having, you know, just uh the widest distribution. And in particular, we have something called uh Jupiter Verified, which we actually just launched earlier today, but it's based on the strict list that we've maintained for a long time. Anyone who is issuing an asset on-chain or sta coming on-chain for the first time understandably is concerned about the possibility of scams and impostor tokens and so forth. So we end up being a first stop just to ensure that the asset is verified correctly, the data is correct, and so on and so forth. And then that allows us to kind of open up the conversation for uh how do we get them integrated into our RFQ solution, for example, which is a way of ensuring that market makers can sell directly with no slippage and no gas costs uh to retail users. That's what's being used by a lot of the tokenized equities folks because they need to either have KYC or something else. So on the trading side, I think we end up uh being great partners there. And certainly on the yield side, uh again, that becomes really useful. I think our new peer-to-peer lending protocol uh which we're calling Offer Book and which will go live in Q1 uh will be really popular here because it allows any asset that comes on from any asset issuer or any institution to instantly find yield-bearing opportunities.

Danny Nelson: Who's Jupiter's biggest challenger right now? Like what is the biggest challenge that Jupiter faces in scaling and making its mission uh succeed?

Kash Dhanda: Oh, uh I mean there's no shortage of challenges uh actually. So can I have like uh three challenges? Is that a one?

Danny Nelson: Two.

Kash Dhanda: Two? Yeah, deal. Uh, so I think the first one is uh cross-product synergy, right? Again, the benefit of this kind of ecosystem of founders approach is we have really fast shipping teams at each product. There's more that we can do to kind of bring these to together. Uh, mobile is like a good example recently where the mobile wallet has added in a bunch of great trading features from uh the terminal experience online. Uh, but there's a lot more that we can do there to make sure the products reinforce each other. And then the second one is around storytelling, as I said. I think it is hard to tell a concise story when you are this kind of sprawling conglomerate of different companies. Uh, I think we're getting better at it, but there's still some ways to go.

Danny Nelson: With storytelling, like the language that you use, how do you make sure that all that is is synced across the organization when you have that really thin like federal layer at the top?

Kash Dhanda: It's something that we're getting better at now. And a big shout out to the product comms team uh who's kind of been put in place and is helping to kind of review things across the entire board. Uh, but honestly the the main thing we need is like a a choir book that we can all sing from, right? So we're singing the same hymn. Uh, not just about what the products are and what they do, but really what the end vision of Jupiter is, what the value we're trying to create for users are, uh and how that value might be slightly different for the institutional audience.

Danny Nelson: Because really this isn't just a story about Jupiter. This is like how all of DeFi comports itself.

Kash Dhanda: Correct. Yeah.

Danny Nelson: Great. Well, thank you, Kash. Uh, we'll wrap it up there. I appreciate your time today and everyone check out Jupiter.

Kash Dhanda: Yeah, appreciate it. Thanks everybody.

sources.title: How BAM extends Solana’s Lead: Making Internet Capital Markets A Reality | BAM
sources.snippet:
[Back to Blog](https://bam.dev/blog/)

November 12, 2025 • 8 minute read

# How BAM extends Solana’s Lead: Making Internet Capital Markets A Reality

Lucas Bruder

Jito Labs

Share

- _Solana's long-term success depends on deterministic execution infrastructure that scales transparently as the network grows. BAM exists to ensure that as Solana scales, the execution layer remains transparent, fair, and aligned with the applications and users who drive network growth._
- _BAM provides cryptographically verifiable transparency for sequencing that no other solution can provide: every ordering decision proven, not promised._
- _BAM will help everyone using or building on Solana succeed:_
 - _Applications gain the execution certainty needed to offer tighter spreads and predictable fills through Application Controlled Execution (ACE)._
 - _Traders benefit from superior execution quality that rivals or exceeds other venues._
 - _Validators get open-source infrastructure they can verify. When applications succeed on Solana, validators succeed._

# What Is BAM?

Solana stands alone: the only blockchain where trading SOL is cheaper and faster on-chain than on centralized exchanges. With BAM, that lead widens as apps gain the tools to outprice and out-execute other exchanges. BAM provides the execution infrastructure necessary to make on-chain trading not just competitive with centralized venues, but categorically superior.

The Block Assembly Marketplace (BAM), which Jito Labs revealed in July, represents the evolution toward a more transparent and application-driven transaction processing paradigm. BAM addresses a fundamental challenge that has constrained the network's growth: the inability for applications to provide predictable, high-quality execution guarantees to their users.

# Why Solana's Block Building Needed to Evolve

We’ve detected at least seven different transaction schedulers operating on Solana, each with distinct ordering logic and timing characteristics. This fragmentation creates fundamental uncertainty for applications and traders who depend on predictable execution. Traders cannot determine whether they're receiving fair execution or being systematically disadvantaged by opaque scheduling decisions made by validators with competing interests. The result is wider spreads and reduced liquidity as market makers price in execution uncertainty, while developers face inconsistent environments that undermine their ability to compete with centralized alternatives.

# Why Jito Labs Is Building BAM

Jito Labs helped pioneer trading infrastructure on Solana through our Block Engine. While these systems created significant value for validators and improved transaction execution, we've also watched how they've evolved. We've seen concentration dynamics emerge, observed the pressure toward private orderflow relationships, and recognized that without intervention, Solana risks following the same path toward opacity that degraded Ethereum's execution layer.

Ethereum's experience with proposer-builder separation (PBS) offers particularly instructive lessons. While PBS introduced competitive block building, it created perverse incentives: builders maximize MEV extraction to win block auctions, often at users' expense. This dynamic leaves the network stuck in a positive feedback loop that can be hard to unwind. Increased sandwich attacks and other forms of extractive MEV pushed orderflow private, as users sought protection through direct builder relationships. Builders offered rebates for exclusive access to this orderflow, and those with the most private flow could pay higher auction fees, winning more blocks and attracting even more exclusive deals. The result is a concentration of power among a small number of builders, increased opacity in transaction processing, and timing games that degraded network performance. Most concerningly, some dominant builders were operated by trading firms with direct financial incentives to exploit

sources.title: Introducing Drift v3: Built to Outperform  | Drift Updates
sources.snippet:
Introducing Drift v3: Built to Outperform  | Drift Updates
[![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6310e7dee49f0866da8eed4c/6867a1f64ca2c75f0d14f371_16c97ce9017b907297827c5113a068f9_Frame%201597880158.avif)](https://www.drift.trade/)
[
Launch App
](#)
## Introducing Drift v3: Built to Outperform
December 4, 2025
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/6310e7dee49f08e5798eed50/693023e13e4b72ada17e0fe2_2025-12-03%2017.19.13.jpg)
[
Contents
](#)
[
Contents
](#)
[
](https://twitter.com/driftprotocol)[
](https://discord.com/invite/fMcZBH8ErM)
[
Trade Now
](https://app.drift.trade/)
![]()
![]()
Drift v3 is the protocol’s biggest performance upgrade yet. Rebuilt around trader execution, v3 delivers**10x faster fills, 10x improved liquidity, and a cleaner, more intuitive interface**than v2.
This upgrade was built for one thing: outperformance. Instant execution. Deeper liquidity. A suite of tools that helps you trade faster and with more confidence.
Drift v2 was the first to bring cross-margin and multi-asset collateral to DeFi on-chain trading. We built on Solana to deliver NASDAQ-level performance on-chain and with major Solana upgrades like Alpenglow, Firedancer and Jito’s Block Assembly Market (BAM), Drift is built to outperform as the network scales.
Since launch, Drift has surpassed 1.5B in TVL, $133B+ in total trading volume and $650M in open interest. Drift v3 is built to take those numbers and your trading even further.
**Drift v3 will be available on Dec 4, 2025.**New users will need an invite code to access Drift v3.
**DM**[**@DriftProtocol**](https://x.com/DriftProtocol)**to request access.**
## Why v3?
Drift v3 represents the protocol’s next stage of maturity: the point where years of infrastructure, liquidity, and execution improvements converge into a faster, clearer, more reliable trading experience.
**Over the past year, Drift has evolved in three critical areas:**
1. Execution speed
2. Liquidity depth
3. Trader experience## How Drift v3 outperforms
*The road to v3 was focused on improving liquidity, trading execution and speed. Here’s what that looks like in practice:*
### **Trading Execution and Speed**
* **10x faster order fills:**On Drift v3, 85% of market orders are filled within the same slot. A slot is Solana’s smallest time unit (about 400 milliseconds). On Drift, that means that trades are executed in under half a second. Instant execution.
* **15x faster take profit/stop loss triggers:**For BTC, it dropped from 15 slots (\~6 seconds) to 1 slot (\~0.4 seconds). This means your risk controls and exits now trigger immediately, reducing slippage and missed opportunities.
* **Gasless trading by default:**Unlike other DEXs, where you pay gas on every trade, Drift abstracts gas fees so that’s one less thing to worry about eating into profits
* **400ms oracle updates**: Prices update every 400 millisecond (roughly 2.5 times per second). This keeps quotes accurate and reduces the chance of stale pricing.
* **Scales with Solana:**As Solana increases block size and throughput, Drift automatically gets faster too, handling more trades per second.### **Liquidity**
* **10x reduction in slippage on market orders:**In Drift v2, large market orders could move the price by 20 bps. In v3, that’s been cut to just 2 bps. Traders now get far better pricing, especially on size.
* **5x tighter TP/SL execution:**Take-profit and stop-loss orders are far more precise. On major markets like SOL and BTC, fills are 4x tighter, while non-majors see up to 5x improvement. This means exits trigger closer to the price you set, reducing losses and protecting gains.
* **Introduction of Drift Liquidity Provider (DLP)**: Provides liquidity for both Perps and Spot markets. Community members can deposit funds into DLP to support market-making and earn a share of the pool's profits. Currently in the testing stage. Public launch in Q1 2026.
* **Expanded Market Maker Program:**With v3’s expanded market maker initiatives, long-tail altcoins now hav

tags.impact_reason: Interview covers Jupiter's new features, institutional interest, and organizational structure, indicating solid updates but not ecosystem-shifting impact.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a modular supercomputer, or 'Engine Core', as the anchor because it visually represents Jupiter's strategy of M&A and integration. The story is about building institutional infrastructure by acquiring and plugging in companies like Rain.fi, which is depicted as a new server blade being slotted into the main chassis. I included the text 'API' as a holographic label because the transcript heavily emphasizes API adoption as the key to onboarding institutional clients and their products.
visual_idea.description: A monumental, monolithic server rack, representing Jupiter's core infrastructure, stands in a vast, dark data center. A sleek robotic arm is precisely inserting a new, glowing amber server blade—representing the Rain.fi acquisition—into an open slot. Beside the port, the word 'API' glows as a crisp, blue holographic label, illuminating the surrounding connections. The background fades into rows of similar server architecture, pierced by beams of clean, volumetric light, creating a sense of immense scale and future growth.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A monumental, monolithic server rack, representing Jupiter's core infrastructure, is constructed from dark, brushed aluminum with a subtle, fine-grained texture that diffuses ambient light, standing towering in a vast, dark data center. The colossal structure emphasizes immense verticality, reaching high into the frame with significant negative space above, reinforcing its monumental scale. A sleek robotic arm, also crafted from brushed aluminum with precise articulation, extends with extreme accuracy, carefully inserting a new, glowing amber server blade—representing the Rain.fi acquisition—into an open slot on the rack. This server blade emits a soft, vibrant holographic glow, reminiscent of a dynamic data stream, illuminating its immediate surroundings. Beside the insertion port, the word 'API' glows as a crisp, luminous blue holographic label, shimmering slightly and casting a distinct blue light onto the adjacent connections and the brushed aluminum surface. The background recedes into an infinite expanse of similar server architecture, suggesting endless growth and complexity, all dramatically pierced by powerful, clean beams of volumetric light that cut through the darkness, creating visible rays and a profound sense of an engine core throbbing with immense energy. The atmosphere is dark, high-contrast, and futuristic, evoking a sense of epic scale and future technological advancement, with sophisticated holographic datastreams flowing through a massive, powerful core. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, cinematic contrast, realistic materials, ultra-photorealistic, vertical composition, 3:4 aspect ratio.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a modular supercomputer, or 'Engine Core', as the anchor because it visually represents Jupiter's strategy of M&A and integration. The story is about building institutional infrastructure by acquiring and plugging in companies like Rain.fi, which is depicted as a new server blade being slotted into the main chassis. I included the text 'API' as a holographic label because the transcript heavily emphasizes API adoption as the key to onboarding institutional clients and their products.
visual_idea.description: A macro, detailed shot focusing on a cluster of thick, interwoven fiber-optic cables plugging into a port on the server's brushed aluminum chassis. A powerful cyan light pulses from within the connection point, signifying the immense flow of data and capital. The focus is on the precision engineering and raw power of the connection itself.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A hyperrealistic, photoreal, macro, and tightly framed shot with extreme detail, focusing on a cluster of thick, interwoven fiber-optic cables plugging into a port on a server's brushed aluminum chassis. The brushed aluminum texture is highly visible, showcasing realistic material properties and subtle reflections. A powerful, volumetric cyan light pulses intensely from within the connection point, creating a holographic datastream effect that signifies the immense flow of data and capital. The light casts dramatic, cinematic contrast and high dynamic range, highlighting the raw power and precision engineering of the connection itself. The composition is square (1:1), centrally focused, filling the entire frame with intricate textures and the vibrant glow. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, cinematic lighting, ultra-photorealistic.

editorial.headline: Jupiter Hires KKR Strategist, Acquires Rain.fi in Aggressive Institutional Pivot
editorial.standfirst: The retail 'Superapp' is growing up. With a private equity veteran at the helm and a new peer-to-peer lending arm, Jupiter is building the infrastructure to onboard Wall Street's capital.
key_takeaways: Hired Xiao-Xiao Zhu (formerly KKR Digital Asset Strategy) as President to lead institutional expansion.
Acquired lending protocol Rain.fi to launch 'Offer Book,' a P2P lending platform arriving Q1 2026.
Formalized 'Ecosystem of Founders' strategy, now comprising 8 acquired teams operating as semi-autonomous units.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
If Solana is the "people's chain," **Jupiter** is rapidly positioning itself as its institutional banking layer. In a definitive signal to the market at Breakpoint 2025, the protocol announced the hiring of **Xiao-Xiao Zhu**—formerly the lead for digital asset strategy at **KKR**—as its new President. The move marks a stark departure from the typical crypto-native hiring playbook. Jupiter isn't just recruiting for code; it is recruiting for capital allocation. Zhu’s mandate is clear: translate the protocol’s deep liquidity and retail dominance into a language that the world’s top 50 credit funds and asset issuers can understand.

This institutional pivot is underpinned by an aggressive M&A strategy that effectively turns Jupiter into a conglomerate. The protocol confirmed its acquisition of **Rain.fi**, a fixed-term lending protocol, whose team will spearhead the development of **Offer Book**. Slated for launch in Q1 2026, Offer Book is a peer-to-peer lending marketplace designed to offer the predictable, slippage-free execution that large-scale market makers and tokenized asset issuers demand. By integrating this directly into Jupiter's massive distribution network, the platform aims to solve the "cold start" liquidity problem for new institutional assets entering the ecosystem.

Operationally, Jupiter is evolving into a federation of specialized products rather than a monolithic app. Co-founder **Kash Dhanda** described the structure as an "Ecosystem of Founders," where acquired teams—like **Gam Olivier**'s squad from **Sonar Watch** and now the Rain.fi team—retain significant autonomy over their verticals. This "states' rights" model allows Jupiter to ship with startup speed across multiple fronts—from mobile trading to portfolio management—while a thin federal layer ensures the narrative remains cohesive. For institutional investors, the message is promising: Jupiter is building a unified financial suite that pairs the speed of Solana with the rigorous verification and execution standards of traditional finance.
